nativeRange.habitat: Outer reef slopes and drop-offs in current-swept areas, typically at depths of roughly 12–81 m

diet.notes: The Watanabei Angelfish belongs to Genicanthus, the only genus within the angelfish family Pomacanthidae whose members feed primarily on zooplankton — tiny drifting animals suspended in the water column — rather than grazing on corals or sponges. In the wild, individuals have been documented picking plankton from the current above reef slopes. In aquariums, a varied diet is generally advised: vitamin-enriched mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty foods, Spirulina, and marine algae preparations, offered several times per day.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Jokuyken15; 4604x3069. Sharp in-water shot of a female showing the diagnostic light-blue body, black-edged dorsal/anal fins and lyretail, and dark bar across the head/eye. Only commercial-friendly image found; all iNat/GBIF results were NonCommercial.

summary: The Watanabei Angelfish is a sexually dimorphic, peaceful planktivore from the tropical Pacific, generally considered reef-safe and commonly advised for larger reef aquariums with strong current and open swimming space.
careNotes: The Watanabei Angelfish belongs to the only planktivorous genus within the angelfish family Pomacanthidae, which is why it is generally considered safer around corals and sessile invertebrates than most other marine angelfishes. The species is strongly sexually dimorphic — males and females look quite different from one another. Males display a pale blue-grey body marked with about eight horizontal black stripes on the lower two-thirds, while females are overall light blue with a wide black bar across the head and a black spot on the snout. In the wild the species tends to form harems of 2–5 individuals, with one dominant male and several females, on outer reef slopes and drop-offs swept by strong current. Only one male should be kept per aquarium, as males have been known to fight to the death. A male–female pair, or a single male kept with several females, is the recommended configuration. The Watanabei Angelfish generally requires ample swimming space and open water above the rockwork; a tank with a long footprint and good flow is commonly recommended. The species has been reported to be susceptible to swim-bladder issues during shipping, so a dim, low-stress acclimation environment is generally advised.
breedingNotes: The Watanabei Angelfish is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning individuals begin life as females and the dominant female in a group may transition into a male if no male is present. In the wild, groups follow a lek-style mating arrangement — a system in which dominant males hold territories and compete to attract females — rather than forming stable pairs. Captive breeding has been reported but remains uncommon in the hobby; the species produces pelagic eggs that drift freely in the water column and are difficult to rear through to juveniles.

additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (15.0 cm TL), preferred temperature (24.2–28.7°C, mean 26.5°C), depth (21–81 m), native range, taxonomy, and trophic level (3.4 ± 0.45, plankton feeder). Confirmed harems of 2–5 individuals. Temperature averaged with LiveAquaria's 22.2–25.6°C to give 23.2–27.2°C used in the file.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Pomacanthidae, Acanthuriformes), max length 15 cm, distribution Taiwan east to Tuamotus including Great Barrier Reef, depth 12–81 m, protogynous hermaphroditism, and lek-style mating system.

fish.breedingNotes: The Watanabei Angelfish is a protogynous hermaphrodite that uses a lek mating system in the wild. Captive breeding has been reported but remains uncommon. The species produces pelagic eggs that drift in the water column and are difficult to rear.
